risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The 23 health care centers were stratified into two strata; rural (12 health care centers) and urban (11 health care centers). Each stratum was allocated into six blocks consisting of two health care centers, in which one was randomly assigned to implement the care manager function." Comment: This describes a stratified block randomization process with centers randomly assigned within blocks, which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

Summary: The paper describes cluster randomization of 23 primary care centres into blocks and states that one centre in each block was randomly assigned to implement the care manager function, but it does not describe any concealment mechanism for those enrolling/recruiting centres or patients (e.g., central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, or similar). Comment: Because the report gives randomization details but no explicit method preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment, allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk; nor is there evidence of a clearly open or predictable allocation process, so the domain is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "concealment of the intervention status of the PCC was not possible, and all personnel at the intervention PCCs were thoroughly informed about the aim of the study and the care manager organization." Comment: This was an open-label cluster trial with no participant/personnel blinding possible. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect key outcomes here, especially patient-reported depressive symptoms, quality of life, satisfaction, and return-to-work/sick-leave related outcomes, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Summary: The main depression and QoL outcomes were patient self-report measures (MADRS-S, BDI-II, EQ-5D, satisfaction questionnaire), so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor. The paper states that "The 3 months assessment at the intervention PCCs was carried out by research personnel unknown to the patient, as it could have been a possible source of bias if the assessment was made by the local care manager. At the control PCCs, the 3 months assessment was administered by a specially trained research nurse." Although explicit blinding is not reported, the assessments were done by research personnel rather than the treating care manager, and the key self-reported symptom outcomes are not plausibly influenced by assessor knowledge in the same way as clinician-rated outcomes. Objective outcomes such as sick leave/return to work and medication were also collected from records/questionnaire, which are less susceptible to detection bias. Overall, there is no clear evidence that outcome measurement was biased by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "At the 3 month follow-up, 86% participated: 79% at the intervention PCCs and 93% at control PCCs, and at 6 months 76% and 83%, respectively" and "A total of 34 patients did not participate in the 3 and 6 months follow-up, 29 in the intervention group and 5 in the control group." Comment: Attrition was moderate and somewhat higher in the intervention arm, but overall follow-up remained reasonably good and the paper indicates that missing participants were younger and had somewhat higher baseline depression scores rather than dropouts clearly linked to treatment failure or adverse events. The authors also state that "The attrition rate was low, and through access to the electronic patient records, complementary data especially concerning medication and sick certification were also collected," and the main analyses used mixed models with repeated measures, which is an appropriate approach for missing outcome data. Overall, the amount and handling of missingness are unlikely to have materially biased the results.
